narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 67% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the 3rd decile for Year 1 school grades (compared to a typical 6th decile), dropping to the 1st decile for Year 3 (far below the average 6th decile), an LSAT score of 36 (a bit under the usual 37), an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 (lower than the average 3.2), upper-middle family income, identifying as female, White, and planning full-time studies. The model weighed these factors to predict bar passage, and unfortunately, every aspect nudged the outcome toward failure rather than success. The most influential was your Year 1 school decile, as being below average there strongly signals challenges in early academic performance linked to bar struggles. Right behind it was your Year 3 decile in the bottom tier, which had an even stronger pull toward predicting failure since it shows persistent underperformance compared to peers. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, with its slight dip below average contributing noticeably to the risk assessment. Family income came next, where upper-middle status didn't provide the boost seen in some higher brackets that correlate with passing. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, as the below-average mark reinforced concerns about sustained academic strength. Race, full-time enrollment plans, and gender had progressively smaller roles, all still tilting toward higher failure odds in your specific case. Overall, your school deciles stood out as the top drivers—far more impactful than test scores or background—because they're well below what successful bar takers typically show. Typical students who pass have much higher deciles around the 5th to 7th, stronger LSATs and GPAs, and profiles that align more favorably across the board. To boost your future bar chances, focus on the most changeable factors like retaking the LSAT for a higher score or building study habits to overcome the academic patterns from your school years, perhaps through targeted prep courses. With effort on these, you could shift closer to the profiles of those who succeed on the first try. Keep pushing forward—you have the potential to rewrite this story.
